A Secure Audio Watermarking Framework Using Frequency-Domain Embedding and Cryptographic Signature Verification

Project Report  |  School of Artificial Intelligence, Amrita Vishwa Vidhyapeetham, Coimbatore
Ishwarya Murugesan Kolisetty Himavarshini Meghana K Ranjith Raja B

Table of Contents

  1. Abstract
  2. Introduction
  3. Literature Review
  4. Methodology
  5. Implementation
  6. Results and Discussion
  7. Conclusion
  8. References

Abstract

Digital audio distribution through online platforms has increased the risk of unauthorized duplication, tampering, and misuse of multimedia content. This report presents a robust and secure audio watermarking framework that integrates signal processing with cryptographic techniques. The method generates a cryptographic identity of the audio using the SHA-256 hashing algorithm, signed with the Ed25519 digital signature scheme to ensure authenticity and integrity. Reed-Solomon error correction coding is applied to the watermark payload for robustness, and encoded watermark bits are embedded into mid-frequency FFT coefficients using a spread-spectrum strategy. Experimental results demonstrate high imperceptibility (SNR > 35 dB), low bit error rate (BER < 0.02 under moderate attacks), and successful cryptographic authentication, making the framework suitable for secure multimedia distribution and digital rights management applications.

Keywords: Audio Watermarking, FFT, SHA-256, Ed25519, Reed-Solomon, Spread Spectrum, Digital Rights Management, Multimedia Security

1. Introduction

The rapid growth of digital multimedia distribution through online platforms has significantly increased the need for reliable mechanisms to protect digital audio content from unauthorized duplication, tampering, and redistribution. Unlike traditional analog media, digital audio files can be copied, modified, and transmitted without degradation in quality, making them highly vulnerable to piracy and manipulation. Ensuring the authenticity, ownership, and integrity of digital multimedia content has therefore become an important challenge in modern multimedia communication systems.

Digital watermarking has emerged as an effective technique for embedding imperceptible information within multimedia signals for purposes such as copyright protection, authentication, and tamper detection. In audio watermarking, hidden information is embedded into the host audio signal such that perceptual quality remains unaffected while allowing reliable extraction of the embedded data when required.

Three Fundamental Requirements of any successful audio watermarking system:
(1) Imperceptibility - the watermark must not degrade perceptual audio quality;
(2) Robustness - the watermark must survive common signal processing operations;
(3) Security - the watermark must resist unauthorized forgery or removal.

Transform-domain watermarking techniques have been widely explored to achieve improved robustness and perceptual transparency. The Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) is commonly used to analyze the spectral components of audio signals and embed watermark information within selected frequency coefficients. However, many existing approaches lack strong mechanisms for verifying the authenticity and integrity of the embedded watermark information, and synchronization errors during extraction can lead to unreliable detection in practical environments.

This work proposes a unified framework that addresses these challenges by combining frequency-domain watermark embedding with cryptographic identity generation and error-resilient recovery. The main contributions are as follows:

C1
Cryptographic Watermark Framework

A robust audio watermarking system combining transform-domain signal processing with SHA-256 hashing and Ed25519 digital signatures for cryptographic authentication.

C2
Reed-Solomon Error Correction

Integration of RS(159,109) coding with 50 parity symbols, enabling reliable recovery of the embedded watermark payload under moderate signal distortions.

C3
Spread-Spectrum FFT Embedding

Multiplicative spread-spectrum embedding using PN sequences seeded by owner identity in the 1-8 kHz mid-frequency band, balancing perceptual transparency with extraction robustness.

C4
Multi-Offset Synchronization Detection

An 8-step offset search strategy supporting reliable watermark detection even after minor time-domain shifts during transmission.

C5
End-to-End Authentication Pipeline

A complete modular embedding and detection pipeline for secure multimedia authentication and digital rights management applications.

2. Literature Review

Table 1 summarizes key contributions from existing audio watermarking research, spanning spread-spectrum methods, transform-domain approaches, synchronization-based detection, and modern deep-learning-based frameworks. While these methods improve robustness and imperceptibility, many lack integrated cryptographic authentication and error correction strategies, motivating the development of the proposed framework.

Ref.YearTechniqueKey IdeaGap / Limitation
[1]2025Survey of conventional audio watermarkingHighlights trends, robustness/imperceptibility trade-offs, attack models and evaluation practiceNo end-to-end authenticated pipeline
[2]2024Deep watermarking via adversarial perturbationDifferentiable attack modeling improves training-time robustnessNo cryptographic identity layer
[3]2022Sync mechanism via spectrum distributionImproves blind detection alignment under editsWatermark not bound to signer identity
[4]2021FFT substitution (mid-frequency embedding)Parity/coefficient relations reduce distortionSignal-domain only; no provenance proof
[5]2025Mode modulation + SVD (Applied Acoustics)Better payload/robustness with denoising + SVD embeddingNo certificate-style verification or ECC binding
[6]2025Fragment energy relationship (TDFER)Resists de-synchronization attacks via fragment energy encodingNo cryptographic attestation of issuer
[7]1997Spread-spectrum watermarking frameworkClassic SS principles and security argumentsNo modern sync/ECC/signature integration
[8]2003SS watermarking for audio (IEEE TSP)Audio-specific masking and desynchronization robustnessNo authenticated ownership proof
[10]2015High-capacity SS method (IEEE/ACM TASLP)Improved payload while maintaining imperceptibilityNo end-to-end hash+signature+ECC pipeline

The proposed framework is the first to tightly integrate spread-spectrum mid-frequency FFT embedding with a structured cryptographic payload (SHA-256 + Ed25519 + MAGIC/VERSION protocol headers), Reed-Solomon forward error correction, and multi-offset synchronization detection in a single cohesive system, addressing all identified limitations simultaneously.

3. Methodology

The proposed system consists of two main phases: the watermark embedding phase and the watermark detection phase. The embedding phase generates a cryptographically secure watermark payload and embeds it into the frequency domain of the audio signal. The detection phase extracts the embedded information and verifies the authenticity of the audio content. The complete pipeline is shown below.

1
Audio Preprocessing

Convert input audio to mono, resample to 44.1 kHz, and peak-normalize to [-1, 1]. This ensures deterministic SHA-256 hashing independent of source format.

2
Cryptographic Identity (SHA-256 + Ed25519)

Compute SHA-256 hash of canonical audio bytes; sign the signable block (MAGIC || VERSION || OWNER_ID || HASH) with Ed25519 private key.

3
Payload Construction (109 bytes)

Assemble: MAGIC (4B) || VERSION (1B) || OWNER_ID (8B) || SHA-256 HASH (32B) || Ed25519 SIGNATURE (64B).

4
Reed-Solomon Encoding (159 bytes)

Encode payload with RS(159,109) over GF(2^8), adding 50 parity symbols. Corrects up to 25 byte errors (~15.7% of encoded stream).

5
Spread-Spectrum FFT Embedding

Split audio into 4096-sample non-overlapping frames. In each frame, embed watermark bits by multiplicatively modifying FFT magnitudes in the 1-8 kHz band using PN chip sequences (31 chips/bit, strength alpha=0.35).

6
Detection and Authentication

Multi-offset sync search (8 steps) - FFT log-magnitude correlation extraction - RS decode - parse payload - Ed25519 signature verify - return DetectionResult.

3.1 Audio Preprocessing

The input audio signal is first normalized and segmented into fixed-length frames. Let the discrete-time audio signal be x[n], n = 0, 1, ..., N-1. The signal is divided into frames of length L:

x_k[n] = x[n + kL],    0 <= n < L

where k denotes the frame index. Table 2 summarizes the standardization parameters applied before watermark generation.

#ParameterStandard ValuePurpose
1Audio FormatWAV (uncompressed)Lossless representation; avoids compression artifacts
2Sampling Rate44,100 HzConsistent frequency representation across inputs
3Channel ConfigMono (stereo merged)Simplifies processing; deterministic hashing
4Bit Depth16-bit PCM (float32 internally)Sufficient dynamic range; wide compatibility
5Amplitude NormPeak-normalized [-1, 1]Prevents clipping; consistent embedding strength

3.2 Cryptographic Identity Generation

To generate a unique fingerprint of the audio signal, the SHA-256 cryptographic hash function is applied to the canonical audio byte representation:

H = SHA256(x)    (256-bit / 32-byte digest)

The hash is then signed using the Ed25519 digital signature scheme over the structured signable block:

S = Sign_sk(MAGIC || VERSION || OWNER_ID || H)

where sk denotes the private signing key and S is a 64-byte signature. This ensures the watermark payload is securely linked to the content creator's identity. Any modification to the audio produces a completely different hash, causing signature verification to fail during extraction.

3.3 Payload Construction

The watermark payload is a 109-byte structured binary record:

P = MAGIC (4B) || VERSION (1B) || OWNER_ID (8B) || HASH (32B) || SIGNATURE (64B)

The MAGIC field ("AWMK") enables fast validity checking during extraction. The VERSION field allows future protocol extensions. Together, hash and signature provide both integrity detection (any change changes the hash) and ownership authentication (only the private key holder could produce S).

3.4 Reed-Solomon Error Correction

Reed-Solomon coding over GF(2^8) with 50 parity symbols expands the payload to 159 bytes, forming the encoded codeword C = RS(159, 109):

Error correction capacity: floor(50/2) = 25 byte errors (~15.7% of codeword)

The 159-byte encoded payload is then serialized to 1,272 bits for spread-spectrum embedding. The RS layer acts as the primary defense against channel noise and signal processing distortions, allowing the cryptographic layer to operate on reliably recovered payloads.

3.5 Spread-Spectrum FFT Embedding

Each 4096-sample audio frame is transformed using the FFT. Watermark bits are embedded by multiplicatively modifying magnitudes in the 1-8 kHz band using owner-specific PN chip sequences of length 31:

|X'_k[m]| = |X_k[m]| * (1 + alpha * b * pn[m - bin_low])

where b in {-1, +1} maps the watermark bit, pn is a (+/-1) chip sequence generated deterministically as pn = f(SHA256(OWNER_ID || bit_index)), and alpha = 0.35 is the embedding strength. Phase is preserved. The mid-frequency band (1-8 kHz) provides a balance between robustness against low-pass filtering attacks and perceptual imperceptibility. With 31 chips per bit and 650 available bins, each frame carries up to 20 watermark bits, requiring a minimum of approximately 64 frames (about 5.9 seconds at 44.1 kHz) to embed the full 1,272-bit payload.

3.6 Watermark Extraction and Authentication

During detection, embedded bits are recovered via log-magnitude correlation with the PN sequences across matched frequency bins:

corr = (1/C) * sum((log|X[k]| - mean) * pn[k]),   bit = 1 if corr > 0

To tolerate minor time-domain shifts, the detector tries 8 equally-spaced sample offsets within a single hop window. After RS decoding, the extracted hash H and signature S are verified:

Verify_pk(MAGIC || VERSION || OWNER_ID || H, S) -> authentic / tampered

If the signature is valid and the MAGIC/VERSION fields parse correctly, the audio is declared authentic with confirmed owner identity and content hash.

4. Implementation

The framework is implemented in Python with a modular architecture. Each component is an independent module, enabling reproducible experimentation, straightforward debugging, and scalability to new audio formats or cryptographic backends.

ModuleFileResponsibility
Audio I/Oaudio_io.pyLoad WAV files; convert to canonical float32 mono at 44.1 kHz via scipy resample
Configurationconfig.pyWatermarkConfig dataclass with all tunable parameters and derived properties (payload_size, total_bits, etc.)
Cryptographycrypto.pyEd25519 key generation/PEM persistence, SHA-256 audio hashing, sign/verify operations
Payloadpayload.pyBuild and parse the 109-byte structured payload; signable_data() for consistent signing scope
ECCecc.pyRS(159,109) encode/decode via reedsolo library over GF(2^8)
Framingframing.pyNon-overlapping 4096-sample frame analysis and copy-back synthesis (avoids double-window artifacts)
Spread Spectrumspread_spectrum.pyPN sequence generation (SHA-256 seeded); per-frame FFT magnitude embedding and log-magnitude correlation extraction
Embedderembedder.pyHigh-level orchestration of the embedding pipeline
Detectordetector.pyMulti-offset detection loop, RS decode, payload parse, signature verification, DetectionResult dataclass
Attacksattacks.pyAWGN, low-pass filter, resample, amplitude scale, time shift, and lossy compression simulation

4.1 Embedding Algorithm

# Algorithm 1 -- Watermark Embedding Procedure # Input: canonical audio x[n], owner_id (8 bytes), private_key (Ed25519) # Output: watermarked audio x'[n] audio_hash = SHA256(x.tobytes()) # 32 bytes signable = MAGIC + VERSION + owner_id + audio_hash # 45 bytes signature = Ed25519.sign(private_key, signable) # 64 bytes payload = MAGIC + VERSION + owner_id + hash + sig # 109 bytes encoded = RS_encode(payload, nsym=50) # 159 bytes / 1272 bits bits = bytes_to_bits(encoded) for frame_k in non_overlapping_frames(x, size=4096): F = rfft(frame_k) for bit b at global_index i: pn = PN_sequence(owner_id, i, chips=31) # deterministic +/-1 b_pm = 2*b - 1 # {0,1} -> {-1,+1} |F[bin_low + i*31 : bin_low + (i+1)*31]| *= (1 + 0.35 * b_pm * pn) frame_k_out = irfft(F) watermarked = copy_back(frame_k_out, original=x) return watermarked

4.2 Detection Algorithm

# Algorithm 2 -- Watermark Detection and Verification # Input: received audio y[n], owner_id, public_key # Output: DetectionResult (found, owner_id, audio_hash, confidence) for offset in [0, hop/8, 2*hop/8, ..., 7*hop/8]: # sync search bits, conf = extract_via_log_corr(y[offset:], owner_id) try: decoded = RS_decode(bits_to_bytes(bits)) # error correction parsed = parse_payload(decoded) # check MAGIC/VERSION signable = MAGIC + VERSION + owner_id + parsed['hash'] if Ed25519.verify(public_key, signable, parsed['sig']): return DetectionResult(found=True, owner_id=..., confidence=mean(conf)) except ReedSolomonError: continue # try next offset return DetectionResult(found=False, reason="No valid watermark found")

4.3 Configuration Parameters

ParameterValueEffect on System
frame_size4,096 samplesFrequency resolution; larger = finer spectral control, more bits/frame possible
hop_size4,096 samplesNon-overlapping; avoids double-windowing artifacts during detection
freq_low / freq_high1,000 / 8,000 HzEmbedding band; mid-freq balances imperceptibility vs. robustness to filtering
chips_per_bit31Processing gain ~14.9 dB; higher = more robust but fewer bits per frame
embed_strength (alpha)0.35Magnitude modification factor; primary SNR vs. robustness trade-off knob
rs_nsym50Parity symbols; corrects up to 25 byte errors per codeword
sync_search_steps8Offset steps tried during detection; improves tolerance to minor time-shifts

5. Results and Discussion

The proposed framework was evaluated using standard speech audio signals sampled at 44.1 kHz. Each stage of the pipeline was validated independently, with performance assessed across imperceptibility, robustness, payload capacity, and cryptographic authentication reliability.

5.1 Audio Canonicalization

Before watermark generation, the input audio was standardized to ensure deterministic hashing and consistent signal processing. Stereo channels were merged to mono and the signal was peak-normalized. Figure 1 confirms that canonicalization preserves the energy distribution across frequency components while removing amplitude scaling variations that could otherwise cause hash mismatches across systems.

5.2 Cryptographic Identity and Payload Structure

The SHA-256 hash produces a unique 32-byte fingerprint of the canonical audio. The Ed25519 signature adds a 64-byte authentication token over the structured signable block. Figure 2 shows the hash and signature byte distributions and the 109-byte payload layout. Any single-sample modification to the audio produces a completely different hash value, enabling reliable tamper detection at the cryptographic layer independently of the watermark channel capacity.

5.3 Reed-Solomon Encoding

Reed-Solomon encoding expands the 109-byte payload to 159 bytes, adding 50 parity symbols as shown in Figure 3. This provides error correction capacity of up to 25 byte errors, ensuring reliable recovery of the watermark payload even after moderate signal processing operations. The 31.4% parity overhead represents a deliberate trade-off: the additional redundancy is the primary enabler of successful watermark recovery under all tested attack conditions.

5.4 FFT-Based Spread-Spectrum Embedding

Figure 4 illustrates the spectral modification introduced during watermark embedding. The modifications in the 1-8 kHz embedding band are small relative to the host signal magnitude, maintaining perceptual transparency with SNR > 35 dB. The deterministic PN chip sequences seeded by SHA-256(OWNER_ID || bit_index) ensure that the embedding pattern is reproducible for the legitimate detector but computationally infeasible to infer without the owner ID.

5.5 Watermark Detection and Signature Verification

Figure 5 shows per-frame extraction confidence values remaining well above the detection threshold under both clean and noise-attacked conditions. The bit detection confusion matrix confirms accurate recovery with low error rates. Ed25519 signature verification succeeds across all tested conditions on unmodified and moderately attacked audio, confirming that the combined RS + cryptographic pipeline provides reliable end-to-end authentication.

5.6 Robustness Under Signal Processing Attacks

Figure 6 presents the robustness evaluation across five attack types. Watermark survival rates remain above 88% in all cases, with BER staying below 0.05 for four of five attacks. The amplitude scaling attack produces the best results (survival 0.99, BER 0.00) since it leaves the relative FFT magnitude structure intact. The simulated compression attack is the most challenging due to combined band-limiting and quantization noise, though the RS layer still enables successful payload recovery in most trials.

5.7 Summary of Performance Metrics

Signature VerificationSuccessfulOn clean and moderately attacked audio

6. Conclusion

This report presented a robust and secure audio watermarking framework that integrates signal processing, cryptographic authentication, and error correction techniques to enable secure and reliable audio content verification. The proposed system combines FFT-based spread-spectrum watermark embedding with SHA-256 hashing, Ed25519 digital signatures, and Reed-Solomon error correction to construct a watermark payload that provides both integrity protection and ownership authentication.

Experimental results demonstrated that the proposed method achieves high imperceptibility (SNR > 35 dB) while maintaining reliable watermark recovery under common signal processing attacks including noise addition, low-pass filtering, resampling, amplitude scaling, and simulated lossy compression. The Reed-Solomon coding stage significantly improves robustness against partial payload corruption, while the Ed25519 cryptographic signature mechanism ensures secure verification of audio authenticity at the application level.

Key Contribution: This is the first framework to tightly integrate spread-spectrum mid-frequency FFT embedding with a structured cryptographic payload (SHA-256 + Ed25519 with MAGIC/VERSION protocol headers), Reed-Solomon forward error correction, and multi-offset synchronization detection in a single cohesive pipeline, addressing limitations of prior works that handle these aspects in isolation.

Future work may explore adaptive embedding strength strategies that modulate the strength parameter alpha based on local frame energy, and machine learning-based watermark detectors trained to be robust against adversarial signal processing attacks. Integration with streaming protocols and real-time digital rights management systems represents a promising direction for practical deployment.
